description: Complete reasoning-chain candidate analysis from gold_standard.jsonl
methodology: Analyzed all 294 TikTok entries. Scored each on: (1) obvious interpretation being wrong, (2) visual/audio evidence contradicting first impression, (3) teaching value for 'reject before commit' principle

tier_1_exceptional.description: Highest teaching value - clear wrong obvious interpretation with strong visual evidence

candidates.obvious_wrong: Complaint 'tastes like 3-year-old made it' is just insult/exaggeration
candidates.evidence_against: Visual reveals actual 3-year-old child made the coffee

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Tastes like 97-year-old made it' is insult about quality
candidates.evidence_against: Visual shows actual 97-year-old man who responds 'I'm doing my best'
candidates.correct: Same pattern - hyperbole literally true, but with sympathy twist

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Short staffed' means understaffed/not enough employees
candidates.evidence_against: Visual shows physically SHORT staff jumping to reach shelves
candidates.correct: Pun on 'short' - they have staff, but they're physically short

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Short staffed' = understaffed
candidates.evidence_against: Same pattern - physically short people trying to reach high shelves

candidates.obvious_wrong: Waitress asking for ID is standard verification
candidates.evidence_against: When customer shows PHOTO of ID, waitress returns with PHOTO of drink
candidates.correct: Waitress mirrors flawed logic - if photo of ID works, photo of drink works

candidates.obvious_wrong: Worker saying shift was 'perfect' means it genuinely
candidates.evidence_against: Robotic voice saying perfect CONTRADICTS visual of frustrated body language
candidates.correct: Irony from forced/fake positive audio against clearly negative visual

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Use your head more' means think harder/be smarter
candidates.evidence_against: Employee physically uses HEAD to do tasks
candidates.correct: Absurdist literal interpretation of figurative speech

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Use your head more' = think harder
candidates.evidence_against: Employees physically perform tasks with their heads

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Use your head' = think smarter
candidates.evidence_against: Worker uses literal head to dispense sauce

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Compliments to the chef' means send praise about food
candidates.evidence_against: Waiter gives personal compliments ('you're handsome') to chef
candidates.correct: Pun on 'compliments' - praise TO vs compliments ABOUT

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Split the bill' means divide payment
candidates.evidence_against: Bartender does physical SPLITS (gymnastics) on floor
candidates.correct: Physical comedy from 'split' as gymnastic action
candidates.current_mechanism: Ordlek, bokstavlig tolkning, subversion av förväntningar

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Can you go lower?' refers to lowering prices
candidates.evidence_against: Employee physically crouches/sits on floor

tier_2_strong.description: Strong teaching value - clear patterns with good visual evidence

candidates.obvious_wrong: Staff calling after overcharged customer seems like honest attempt
candidates.evidence_against: Visual shows deliberately weak calling, then splitting extra money
candidates.correct: The 'attempt' is designed to fail - they want to keep extra

candidates.obvious_wrong: Bartender mishearing '20 euros' as '10 euros' is mistake
candidates.evidence_against: Both bartenders coordinated - 'mishearing' is theater for deal illusion
candidates.correct: Manipulation revealed as coordinated, 10 is actual (high) price

candidates.obvious_wrong: Chef's hunting pose suggests pest/animal under counter
candidates.evidence_against: Reveals hidden beer glass, not animal
candidates.correct: 'Creature hunt' setup subverted - hiding beer at work
candidates.notes: Hunt is a strong word. It plays on the idea of an animal (like a bird) has found itself in the kitchen. So not a hunt, more a responsible action to take care of the situation. And the beer thing makes it silly.

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Finish it' means complete filling the beer glass
candidates.evidence_against: Colleague DRINKS (finishes) entire beer instead
candidates.correct: Pun on 'finish' - complete task vs consume contents

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Finish this for me' means complete making the drink
candidates.evidence_against: Colleague drinks the drink instead
candidates.correct: Same disambiguation - finish task vs finish consuming

candidates.obvious_wrong: Boss's sweeping expectation is about thoroughness
candidates.evidence_against: FEET visible under counter in 'thorough' version - implies hidden body
candidates.correct: Escalation goes beyond thoroughness to dark territory

candidates.obvious_wrong: Internal monologue about tipping is customer's thoughts
candidates.evidence_against: Revealed to be CASHIER speaking out loud to influence tip
candidates.correct: POV flip - what seemed internal was external manipulation
candidates.notes: The customers POV and the sudden change in sound dialogue 'perspective' - meaning who's perspective the words come from, imply that it's an internal dialogue. As well as the POV shot focusing on the screen showing the tipping options. This uses a scenographic technique to infer that it is the POV individuals thoughts.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Bartender showing cocktail shaking is just skill display
candidates.evidence_against: From female colleague's angle, motion looks explicitly sexual
candidates.correct: Camera angle transforms innocent action into suggestive one
candidates.current_mechanism: Subversion, Prank, Ironic build-up, sexuellt laddat tema

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Tack detsamma' (likewise) to 'enjoy meal' is just verbal slip
candidates.evidence_against: Waitress takes it literally and shares food with customer
candidates.notes: Exploitation is not the best word. It's more of an absurdist playing along. Making it that a simple speaking miss suddenly goes to taking it as an invite, like a person that doesn't understand that not everyone is a friend that allows anything at any time.

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Shake it' on drink means shake the container
candidates.evidence_against: Customer interprets as 'shake your body' and dances

candidates.obvious_wrong: Customer complaint about missing chicken is valid
candidates.evidence_against: Solution is flipping container - chicken was there, upside down
candidates.correct: Problem was user error in orientation
candidates.notes: How this could be inferred is an interesting issue. Flipping the box to show that the chicken really is there requires some thinking and inferrence.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Staff will remake order or remove cream normally
candidates.evidence_against: Child 'assistant' eats the unwanted cream

candidates.obvious_wrong: Hand-over-camera will show costume change to weekend clothes
candidates.evidence_against: Clothes remain SAME - no transformation
candidates.correct: LACK of transformation reveals no weekends off

candidates.obvious_wrong: Customer complaint about cold food is valid
candidates.evidence_against: Flashback shows customer photographed food for an hour

candidates.obvious_wrong: Barista gives full drink to customer (professional)
candidates.evidence_against: Behind counter shows colleague getting 'extra' poured in their cup
candidates.notes: Kind of. It also frames the business as a very nonchalant and dream-like opportunistic place where no rules apply. The behaviour is not normal, which adds absurdism and makes one think 'what is really going on here'?

candidates.obvious_wrong: Voiceover describing 'perfect' shift matches reality
candidates.evidence_against: Body language shows complete chaos
candidates.correct: Ironic gap between stated and shown

candidates.obvious_wrong: Chef will use cutting board as surface
candidates.evidence_against: Chef uses cutting board AS cutting implement

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Dice the onion' means small cubes (culinary)
candidates.evidence_against: Chef cuts into literal dice shapes (gaming dice)

candidates.obvious_wrong: Waitress returning after complaints is normal service
candidates.evidence_against: Returns with HAMMER
candidates.notes: This is incorrect. The sketch is showing how a waitress can be very servicable, with a customer rejecting their help/service. Then when the customer sees the manager, 'now' ready to order, she complains that the waitress hasn't done their job correctly. The waitress now comes with a hammer to show an absurdist reaction/hypothetical end of starting a confrontation with a hammer behind her back as a sign of frustration.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Denied customization = customer leaves
candidates.evidence_against: Elderly customer enters kitchen, makes own burger
candidates.notes: This clip is strictly funny on the basis of it's acting and slapstick elements. The tempo and escalating wildness of the scenario makes it engaging.

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Finare' (nicer) serving means better food presentation
candidates.evidence_against: Employee makes THEMSELVES fancier instead
candidates.correct: Object of 'nicer' shifts from food to server

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Take your time' is politeness meaning don't rush
candidates.evidence_against: Employee LITERALLY takes their time, does slow meaningless tasks
candidates.notes: This isn't exactly malicious compliance. It's more a playful/absurdist reaction towards hearing the polite phrase. Seen by the playfulness of the characters and overall soft and inviting tone. If the clip had other actors, different dialogue, the point could potentially be construed to be malicious. But then, the punchline would be to 1. Hear incorrectly 2. Wanting to take advantage of a customer's good will to the help of no one. The rest of the sketches contents would have to be changed to have a fun structure that lifts the malicious intent, if that's the point.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Customer got wrong order (staff error)
candidates.evidence_against: BOTH customer and staff were on phones
candidates.correct: Shared blame due to parallel distraction
candidates.notes: Both are blaming each other. Some of the engaging aspects is to see the concequence and the effect of both of the actors negligence.

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'I know the owner' expects privileges
candidates.evidence_against: Staff provides EXTREME privileges (money, etc.) in ironic overkill

candidates.obvious_wrong: Understanding customer is just polite
candidates.evidence_against: Barista response (removing apron, kissing camera) is wildly disproportionate
candidates.notes: The camera representing the viewers POV, a kiss would be to literally be kissed on the mouth for simple politeness.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Barista attacks customer for 'Venti' - just barista issue
candidates.evidence_against: OWNER also joins attack
candidates.correct: Corporate terminology as cultural offense in local cafe

candidates.obvious_wrong: Pizza was ready, waitress did nothing wrong
candidates.evidence_against: Chef's scream reveals pizza wasn't finished
candidates.correct: 'Ready' means different things to different roles
candidates.notes: The chef 'living in his own world' working on his own pace, placing a pizza in a place where the server may think it's done. When the server takes the food, the chef notices it and gives an instinctive but funny small scream, like a bird or something (not an agressive or scary scream), to indicate his chock of the food going out without being 'topped off', as well as the chefs realization that the server lacks the class of not noticing that the food is not ready. So cultural expectations as well.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Staff rushing after 'hurry' request
candidates.evidence_against: Staff chants 'hurry' without actually speeding up
candidates.notes: The response of the team is pettyness and ineffective action. The team starting clapping in unison shows that it's a bit strange, and absurdist, and probably a bit mean spirited.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Chef's 'two' finger gesture means quantity
candidates.evidence_against: Gen Z interprets as peace sign first
candidates.correct: Same gesture has different generational meanings

candidates.obvious_wrong: Barista will serve pumpkin spice latte
candidates.evidence_against: Barista categorically rejects with hostility, points to exit

candidates.obvious_wrong: Colleague is actually blind
candidates.evidence_against: 'Blindness' is faked to avoid covering shift

candidates.obvious_wrong: Restaurant accepting customers elsewhere is mature
candidates.evidence_against: Visual reveals person bound in car trunk
candidates.correct: Extreme dark humor about 'handling' disloyal customers

candidates.obvious_wrong: Metal in microwave is accident
candidates.evidence_against: Deliberate sabotage to force closure after denied vacation

candidates.obvious_wrong: Spilling drink is minor mistake
candidates.evidence_against: Colleague 'disposed of' in garbage bag, kicked into dumpster

candidates.obvious_wrong: Headphones = focused on work music
candidates.evidence_against: Listening to basic job training tutorial

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'Keep the change' is generous
candidates.evidence_against: Customer manipulated counting to BECOME receiver of money

candidates.obvious_wrong: Reaching for pen is normal
candidates.evidence_against: Poor execution + facial expression makes it look inappropriate
candidates.correct: Same action with poor execution looks inappropriate
candidates.current_mechanism: Absurdism, fysisk humor, kontrast, explicit humor

candidates.obvious_wrong: Server approaching with food will deliver it
candidates.evidence_against: Server swerves to different table

candidates.obvious_wrong: Staff will find normal solution for 50 cent shortage
candidates.evidence_against: Staff EAT parts of food to reduce price

candidates.obvious_wrong: Preparing regular's order in advance is good service
candidates.evidence_against: Regular walks past restaurant
candidates.notes: Also a bit dissapointing for the viewer, seeing how a hopeful and kind gesture falls flat and makes one feel slightly bad for the server.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Man kneeling is proposing marriage
candidates.evidence_against: Presenting poke bowl, not ring

candidates.obvious_wrong: Server reporting missing kebab is honest
candidates.evidence_against: Server is eating the 'missing' kebab
candidates.correct: Error report is cover for theft

candidates.obvious_wrong: Man offering to pay is genuine
candidates.evidence_against: Pays with HER card
candidates.notes: A clever way of making it seem like he's generous, but instead doing the unexpected thing that frames the man as stupid.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Free item for bottle flip is genuine offer
candidates.evidence_against: Cashier sabotages attempt AND raises price for failure
candidates.correct: Game designed to extract money, not give freebies
candidates.notes: The bottle flip concept gives the customer a fun option to get item for free, by flipping the bottle (the game), but the cashier shows a sudden meanness by sabotaging the customers attempt. The 'invitation' to do something fun for the customer is a social invitation that is violated surprisingly, with a tone of meanness by physically hitting away a bottle.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Higher skill = more elegant pan flipping
candidates.evidence_against: At chef level, pan flips BY ITSELF while chef uses phone

candidates.obvious_wrong: Elegant drink delivery is natural
candidates.evidence_against: Behind bar shows awkward leg extension for balance

candidates.obvious_wrong: 'I have tea for you' = offering drink
candidates.evidence_against: When gossip declined, tea withdrawn
candidates.correct: Tea was contingent on gossip acceptance

candidates.obvious_wrong: Gen Z serving burnt food is laziness
candidates.evidence_against: Any correction triggers immediate quitting
candidates.notes: This one is tricky and has layers. The food is burnt and can be attributed to laziness, and any corrections triggers immediate quitting as well. The point of the sketch is to infer that the younger generation are fragile to feedback, showing that they are both nonchalant and unwilling to take feedback. This is a subtle and layered meaning on top of the visuals, that shows some general outlines but requires inferrence based on limited data.

candidates.obvious_wrong: Waitress responds normally to customer call
candidates.evidence_against: Throws entire tray of drinks to respond immediately

candidates.obvious_wrong: Barista offering gossip is friendly
candidates.evidence_against: Customer declines gossip, drink taken back
candidates.correct: Drink and gossip were bundled - no interest = no drink

summary.recommended_for_training: All 90+ (12) plus best from each pattern type = ~25-30 examples

must_include: literal_idiom (3 examples: use your head x3)
phrase_disambiguation (3 examples: split bill, go lower, finish it)
